metadata.title: Detect Collider Bias in a Hiring Pipeline with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to actually see collider bias appear in a hiring pipeline, not just be warned about it. Help me simulate it. Concretely: (1) generate data where skill and luck independently affect getting hired but are uncorrelated in the full population, (2) show me the spurious negative correlation that appears once I condition on the hired subset, and (3) quantify how the estimated skill-luck relationship shifts as I move from the whole applicant pool to only those selected.

props.command: I want to actually see collider bias appear in a hiring pipeline, not just be warned about it. Help me simulate it. Concretely: (1) generate data where skill and luck independently affect getting hired but are uncorrelated in the full population, (2) show me the spurious negative correlation that appears once I condition on the hired subset, and (3) quantify how the estimated skill-luck relationship shifts as I move from the whole applicant pool to only those selected.
props.segment: Detect Collider Bias in a Hiring

beats.narration: Among hired candidates, skill and personality are negatively correlated. That correlation does not exist in the general population. Conditioning on a collider created it.
A collider is a node that receives arrows from two causes. Conditioning on it opens a path that was closed — creating a spurious correlation between the two causes.
We ask Claude to simulate five thousand candidates with independent skill and personality scores, hire anyone where their sum exceeds one, and compute the correlation before and after selection.

props.command:
claude "Simulate 5000 candidates:
  skill ~ N(0,1), personality ~ N(0,1)
  hire if skill+personality > 1
  compute Pearson r:
    full population vs hired subset"

beats.narration: Claude writes the simulation. Population r is near zero — the variables are independent by construction. Hired subset r is approximately negative 0.65 — the collider opened a spurious path.

props.code:
# collider_sim.py — Claude Code output
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
np.random.seed(42)
N = 5000
skill = np.random.randn(N)
personality = np.random.randn(N)

# hire = collider node
hired = (skill + personality) > 1.0

r_pop, _ = stats.pearsonr(skill, personality)
r_sub, _ = stats.pearsonr(skill[hired], personality[hired])

print(f'Population r = {r_pop:.3f}')  # ≈ 0.00
print(f'Hired subset r = {r_sub:.3f}') # ≈ -0.65

beats.narration: Two panels: full population, correlation near zero. Hired subset, correlation minus 0.65. The hiring threshold is the collider. Conditioning on it opened a path between two independent variables.
Now we vary the hiring threshold from strict top ten percent to lenient top sixty percent and observe how collider bias strength changes.

props.command:
claude "Vary hiring threshold:
  strict (top 10%), moderate (top 30%),
  lenient (top 60%), no filter.
  Report hired subset r for each."

beats.narration: Stricter selection creates stronger collider bias. Top ten percent: r equals negative 0.82. Top sixty percent: r equals negative 0.35. No filter: r equals zero. The collider bias is strongest where selection is most restrictive.
The hiring paradox is not an illusion — it is a structural consequence of conditioning on a collider. The correlation only appears in the selected subset because selection created the path.
Next: translating a causal claim across three registers — statistical, causal, and clinical.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.
